desc: Busan is South Korea's looser, saltier counterpoint to Seoul: beaches, seafood markets, hillside neighborhoods, and temple cliffs all fit inside a big port city that still works surprisingly well by metro and short taxi hops. It is strongest when you treat it as a city of contrasting districts rather than one beach stop, mixing coast, food, viewpoints, and one or two quieter cultural anchors.
hls: Good metro coverage for a sprawling city

todo.name: Haeundae shoreline day
todo.desc: Pair the beach with a seafront walk and nearby cafes to understand Busan's most famous coastal district.

todo.name: Jagalchi Market and Nampo area
todo.desc: Use the seafood market and surrounding streets for the city's classic port-side energy and eating culture.

todo.name: Gamcheon Culture Village
todo.desc: Go for the layered alleys, murals, and wartime-settlement history rather than just the photo spots.

todo.name: Haedong Yonggungsa
todo.desc: Make time for the cliffside temple in Gijang, where the sea setting matters as much as the shrine itself.

todo.name: Sunset or evening lookout
todo.desc: Leave space for one skyline-or-coast view after dark to feel the city's scale beyond its individual districts.

todo.name: Seafood-heavy dinner
todo.desc: Busan deserves at least one meal built around raw fish, grilled shellfish, or market-led seafood specialties.

wifi.note: Excellent citywide broadband and mobile connectivity; easy for remote work in hotels, cafes, and transport-heavy itineraries.

prac.fltNote: There is no realistic destination-level nonstop from the repo's London airports into Busan, and current Gimhae service is mostly regional rather than UK-facing. Most London-origin trips either connect once into Gimhae via another Asian or Gulf hub, or use nonstop Heathrow-Seoul service plus a domestic flight or KTX onward; summer beach season and major autumn festival periods create the sharpest hotel and airfare pressure.
prac.lang: Korean is the main language; English appears in major transport hubs and many visitor-facing venues, but not everywhere.
prac.visa: South Korea entry rules depend on nationality and current visa-free or pre-travel authorization arrangements, so check the latest before departure.
bestFor: Spring and autumn East Asia itineraries
alerts: July-August is hot, humid, and rain-prone, with monsoon bursts and occasional typhoon disruption risk.
Busan's most popular beaches and waterfronts get very crowded on Korean summer weekends and festival dates.
Gamcheon is hilly and stair-heavy, so it is less of a casual drop-in than social media makes it look.
Travel times between districts can be longer than expected on a map, so cluster your days geographically.

sweet: April to May and October are the best all-round windows for Busan's coast, markets, and temple outings, while late July to August is better only if you actively want peak beach season despite the humidity and rain risk.
